fast.summary: CONVINV makes conversational dense retrieval models interpretable by inverting opaque session embeddings back into human-readable text using a Vec2Text model, enhanced with external query rewrites. It bridges the gap between black-box embedding representations and transparent query rewriting without sacrificing retrieval performance.
fast.key_idea: Train a Vec2Text inversion model on the shared embedding space of session and query encoders to decode session embeddings into interpretable text, optionally guided by explicit query rewrites.
fast.why_it_matters: Interpretability is a key barrier to debugging and improving conversational search systems; this work lets practitioners see what a session embedding 'means' as natural language, enabling targeted diagnosis and trust in retrieval behavior.
possible_use_cases: Debugging conversational search models by inspecting what session embeddings capture about multi-turn context
Auditing retrieval systems for bias or unexpected behavior by examining the decoded query representations
Improving query rewriting pipelines by using decoded embeddings as soft supervision signals

deep.method_breakdown: CONVINV trains a Vec2Text inversion model to decode opaque conversational session embeddings back into human-readable query text, exploiting the fact that session and ad-hoc query embeddings share the same vector space in conversational dense retrieval systems. The inversion model is trained using the ad-hoc query encoder as a backbone, allowing it to map session embeddings to interpretable text. Additionally, externally generated query rewrites are incorporated as supervision signals to further guide the inversion toward more coherent, contextually grounded interpretations.
deep.key_innovation: The key novelty is bridging the gap between black-box session embeddings and transparent query rewriting by leveraging the shared embedding space between session encoders and ad-hoc query encoders — something prior Vec2Text work did not exploit. Prior interpretability work on dense retrieval focused on single-turn ad-hoc retrieval; CONVINV is the first to tackle multi-turn conversational session embeddings. The rewriting-enhanced inversion is also novel: using external rewrites as additional training signal to steer the decoded text toward interpretable conversational queries.
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design choice is exploiting the shared embedding space between session embeddings and ad-hoc query embeddings. Because conversational dense retrievers are typically built by fine-tuning ad-hoc dense retrievers, their session encoder output lives in the same metric space as the query encoder. This allows a Vec2Text model trained on ad-hoc queries to generalize to session embeddings with minimal domain gap, making the inversion feasible without requiring paired (session embedding, text) training data from scratch.
deep.limitations: The approach inherits Vec2Text's known limitations: inversion quality degrades for long or complex sessions where a single embedding cannot faithfully encode all conversational context. The method also depends on the quality of external query rewrites — noisy or off-target rewrites can degrade interpretability. Furthermore, the shared-space assumption holds for existing architectures but may break for future conversational retrievers that depart significantly from ad-hoc pre-training. Finally, faithfulness to retrieval performance is approximate; the inverted text may shift ranking behavior on edge cases even when aggregate metrics appear preserved.
interview_questions: Why is the shared embedding space between session and ad-hoc query encoders critical to CONVINV's design, and what would break if this assumption did not hold?
How does Vec2Text work, and what modifications or training choices did CONVINV make to adapt it for conversational session embeddings?
What role do external query rewrites play in the inversion process — are they used at training time, inference time, or both, and why does incorporating them improve interpretability?
How do you evaluate whether the inverted text 'faithfully preserves' retrieval performance, and what metrics or benchmarks were used?
What are the broader implications of this work for trustworthy conversational AI — how does interpretability of retrieval embeddings connect to safer or more debuggable search systems?
interview_answers: The shared space is critical because it allows the Vec2Text inversion model — trained using the ad-hoc query encoder — to process session embeddings without a prohibitive domain gap. If session embeddings lived in a disjoint space, the inversion model would have no signal to decode them into meaningful text. The approach would require either a fully paired session-level training corpus (expensive) or a separate architecture, undermining the simplicity of CONVINV.

Vec2Text is a text inversion model that trains an encoder-decoder (e.g., T5) to reconstruct the input text given only its dense embedding, using iterative hypothesis refinement during inference. CONVINV adapts this by using the conversational system's ad-hoc query encoder as the embedding function during training, so the inversion model learns to invert embeddings from that specific encoder family, which is also used for session encoding.

External query rewrites (e.g., from rule-based or LLM-based rewriters) are incorporated as additional training targets or regularization signals, effectively providing interpretable 'anchors' that guide the decoder toward producing fluent, contextually appropriate queries rather than adversarial or degenerate strings that happen to match the embedding. This is used at training time to shape the output distribution toward human-readable rewrites.

Faithfulness is evaluated by encoding the inverted text with the original query encoder and measuring retrieval metrics (NDCG, Recall@k) on standard conversational search benchmarks (e.g., QReCC, TopiOCQA, TREC CAsT) using the inverted text as a proxy query. If retrieval performance matches or closely approximates running the original session embedding directly, the inversion is considered faithful.

Interpretable session embeddings allow practitioners to inspect what the model 'thinks' the user is asking at each turn, enabling targeted debugging when retrieval fails, bias auditing, and user-facing explanations. This connects to the broader trustworthy AI agenda: opaque embeddings make it impossible to know why a retrieval system returns certain results, while CONVINV surfaces an explicit textual hypothesis that can be examined, corrected, or flagged — a foundation for human-in-the-loop oversight of conversational search.
